title: PlasticIceSheet.jl — design & plan
text:
A small, AD-friendly Julia package that reconstructs a perfectly-plastic, steady-state ice-sheet surface from three inputs — a grounded-ice margin/mask, a basal shear stress field τ_b(x,y), and a bed elevation z_b(x,y) — by solving the governing equation as a static Hamilton–Jacobi (eikonal-type) problem on a grid.
It is intended as a complementary first-guess tool alongside a full dynamic model (Yelmo) and an independent 3-D GIA / sea-level solver — e.g. to seed spin-up, to provide an elevation field for an SMB model, or as a relaxation target. It is not a dynamic model and carries no transient, thermomechanics, or ice-shelf physics.


This package is a deliberate simplification and modernization of ICESHEET 2.0 by Evan J. Gowan et al.:

Source code (Fortran): https://github.com/evangowan/icesheet
Local copy analyzed: ~/models/icesheet
Description paper: Gowan, E. J., Tregoning, P., Purcell, A., Lea, J., Fransner, O. J., Noormets, R., and Dowdeswell, J. A. (2016). ICESHEET 1.0: a program to produce paleo-ice sheet reconstructions with minimal assumptions. Geoscientific Model Development, 9(5), 1673–1682. https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-9-1673-2016 (local: ~/models/icesheet/gmd-9-1673-2016.xml)
v2.0 / PaleoMIST application: Gowan et al. (2021), Nature Communications 12, 1199. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-21469-w

ICESHEET is GPL-3.0. This is a clean-room reimplementation of the physics (one published equation, below); none of the Fortran source is copied. This package is MIT-licensed.



The entire scientific content of ICESHEET is one equation. Under the perfectly-plastic, steady-state assumption the basal shear stress τ_b balances the gravitational driving stress τ_d (paper Eq. 5):
τ_b = τ_d = ρ_i g H |∇z_s|,    H = z_s − z_b ≥ 0
with z_s the ice surface elevation, z_b the bed elevation, H the thickness, τ_b the basal shear stress, τ_d the driving stress, ρ_i ice density, g gravity. With τ_b prescribed, rearrange for the surface slope to get the form we solve:
|∇z_s| = τ_b / (ρ_i g H) = τ_b / (ρ_i g (z_s − z_b))
Boundary condition: H = 0 (z_s = z_b) at the margin, plus a marine flotation thickness H_flt = (ρ_w/ρ_i)(z_ss − z_b) where the grounded margin sits below sea level z_ss.
This is a static Hamilton–Jacobi equation. The viscosity solution of an upwind grid scheme automatically produces the correct surface where flow would converge — so saddles, domes, and coalescing ice caps emerge for free, with no flowline tracing.



:flat — flat-bed eikonal (simple case). Neglect bed slope in the flux (|∇z_s| ≈ |∇H|). Substituting u = H² gives a textbook eikonal:
|∇u| = 2τ_b / (ρ_i g),     u = 0 at the margin
Solve, then H = √u, z_s = H + z_b. Exact eikonal; ideal when bed relief ≪ ice thickness (continental interiors). The paper’s own sensitivity tests indicate the bed-gradient terms are second-order except in mountainous terrain.
:full — full Hamilton–Jacobi (default). Accounts for the bed slope ∇z_b. The effective right-hand side (in the w = H² variable) is
|∇w| = √(G² − 4√w(∇z_b·∇w) − 4w|∇z_b|²),    G = 2τ_b / (ρ_i g)
which depends on w through ∇w. The frozen right-hand side is re-solved as a constant-RHS eikonal and iterated to a fixed point (see Implementation notes — a damped Picard iteration is required, as the undamped map oscillates). Correct over arbitrary bed relief; reduces exactly to :flat when ∇z_b = 0.





Fast sweeping (Zhao 2005): Gauss–Seidel with the Godunov upwind Hamiltonian, alternating the four diagonal sweep orderings, iterated to a tolerance on the max update. O(N) per sweep, a handful of sweeps to converge.
Godunov update at node (i,j) with spacing h, neighbor minima a, b:

if |a − b| ≥ F h: u = min(a, b) + F h
else: u = (a + b + √(2 F² h² − (a − b)²)) / 2 where F = G = 2τ_b/(ρ_i g) (flat mode) or the w-dependent effective RHS above (:full).


This replaces the ~2,600-line recursive flowline/crossover/saddle machinery of ICESHEET (find_flowline_fisher_adaptive_4.f90) with a kernel on the order of ~150–250 lines.




Given the intended use (first guess; external GIA; Yelmo for dynamics), we drop:

Method-of-characteristics flowline tracing and all of its bookkeeping: crossover detection / motorcycle-graph algorithm, saddle & dome detection, polygon splitting, recursion, adaptive contour resampling. Subsumed by the grid HJ solver.
The GIA / sea-level iteration loop (CALSEA/SELEN coupling, global/deform/, selen_format.sh, …). The package consumes a bed z_b; the user owns GIA.
The GMT projection/preprocessing pipeline (run.sh, prepare_icesheet.sh, create_ss_grid.f90, shapefile→τ_b domains, reduce_dem, nearest_int, bicubic, diff_map) and the hand-rolled GMT binary-record grid reader (grids.f90). Replaced by in-memory arrays now, NetCDF I/O via a package extension later.
The offshore boundary Nye-smoothing pre-pass — the HJ scheme enforces the maximum surface slope intrinsically.
The iterative τ_b-tuning shell loop (adjust_ss) — replaced by gradient-based optimization of τ_b enabled by AD (see below).





Inputs: grounded-ice mask, bed z_b, shear-stress field τ_b (or a constant / a low-dimensional parameterization).
Plastic physics + marine flotation BC at the grounding line.
Gridded outputs: surface z_s, thickness H, plus reductions (volume, area).
AD-friendliness: the solver is written so that a scalar loss on the output (e.g. misfit to a target surface, or to Yelmo) can be differentiated w.r.t. τ_b, enabling gradient-based inversion of basal shear stress — the modern replacement for ICESHEET’s manual domain tuning.





Steady-state geometry only; biased thick/peaked where the real ice was streaming or dynamic (low effective driving stress the plastic assumption can’t see).
τ_b is the free field carrying essentially all the uncertainty. For a first guess a constant (~50–100 kPa) or a bed-keyed value is fine; don’t over-interpret as a constrained reconstruction.





AD backend & strategy (forward-through-solver vs. implicit/adjoint at the fixed point; Enzyme vs. ForwardDiff vs. Zygote) — depends on whether τ_b is a full per-cell field or a low-dimensional parameterization. To be decided before the core lands.
License — decided: MIT (clean-room physics reimplementation; ICESHEET is GPL-3.0).
NetCDF I/O as a package extension (ext/).





Both modes solve for w = H², not the surface z_s directly: the driving slope τ_b/(ρ_i g H) blows up at the margin (H→0) and biases a direct-z_s solve low. :flat is the plain eikonal |∇w| = 2τ_b/(ρ_i g); :full adds the bed-gradient correction.
:full damping. The :full effective RHS √(G² − 4√w(∇z_b·∇w) − 4w|∇z_b|²) carries ∇w, so “freeze RHS, re-solve” is not contractive — undamped it oscillates over real relief. It is solved by a damped (under-relaxed) Picard iteration (relax, default 0.5). Very steep beds (relief ≈ ice thickness over a few cells) may still not converge.
Reverse-mode AD (differentiable_thickness, extension keyed on ImplicitDifferentiation): implicit differentiation of the converged Godunov residual godunov_residual(w, τ_b) = 0. :flat gradients are exact to machine precision; :full gradients are exact once the damped forward solve has driven the residual to ≈ 0 (hence the firmer n_outer/outer_tol defaults on that path). The forward fast sweep is a black box; only the residual is differentiated (ForwardDiff), so AD cost is independent of sweep/outer-iteration count and scales to a full per-cell τ_b field.
NaN hygiene: √w is floored at H_min² wherever it is differentiated (sqrt(0) has an infinite derivative that otherwise poisons the reverse pass).





Package scaffolding, plan doc
Core HJ / eikonal fast-sweeping solver (:flat, :full)
Forward-mode (ForwardDiff) τ_b-gradients, validated vs finite differences
Reverse-mode (implicit-diff) full-field τ_b-gradients, validated vs ForwardDiff/FD
Tests: Nye analytic profile, flat≡hj on flat bed, residual convergence, AD
NetCDF I/O extension
τ_b-inversion example (examples/invert_tau.jl)
Next: implicit-diff iterative linear solver tuning for very large grids; better :full convergence on steep beds (e.g. Anderson acceleration / a true HJ Godunov for the bed-coupled term); coordinate/projection handling for real datasets

title: Algorithms
text: This page explains, end to end, how PlasticIceSheet.jl turns a margin, a bed, and a basal-shear-stress field into an ice sheet — and how it differentiates the result with respect to the shear stress.

title: Algorithms
text:
Notation

H = z_s - z_b

title: Algorithms
text:
1. The governing equation
The reconstruction assumes perfectly plastic, steady-state ice. In that limit the basal shear stress \tau_b exactly balances the gravitational driving stress \tau_d:

\tau_b = \tau_d .
\tag{1}
The driving stress is set by the ice geometry — for an ice surface elevation z_s, bed elevation z_b, and thickness H = z_s - z_b, with \rho_i the ice density and g gravity:

\tau_d = \rho_i\, g\, H\, |\nabla z_s| .
\tag{2}
Combining Equation 1 and Equation 2 relates the prescribed basal shear stress to the surface geometry:

\tau_b = \rho_i\, g\, H\, |\nabla z_s| .
\tag{3}
In a reconstruction \tau_b is prescribed (it is the field we choose or invert for) and the surface z_s is the unknown. Rearranging for the surface slope gives the equation we actually solve:

|\nabla z_s| = \frac{\tau_b}{\rho_i\, g\, H} = \frac{\tau_b}{\rho_i\, g\, (z_s - z_b)} .
\tag{4}
The right-hand side is a slope (dimensionless). Equation 4 is a static Hamilton–Jacobi equation: the surface is built inward from the margin, where H \to 0. Its upwind (viscosity) solution automatically yields the correct envelope wherever ice converges, so ice divides, domes, and merging ice caps appear without any explicit flowline or saddle handling.
This is the same physics as ICESHEET 2.0, but solved on a grid rather than by tracing characteristics.

title: Algorithms
text:
2. Solve for H^2, not z_s
Near the margin H \to 0, so the driving slope \tau_b/(\rho_i g H) in Equation 4 diverges. Discretising Equation 4 for z_s directly is therefore inaccurate near the margin and biases the whole reconstruction thin. The fix is a change of variable.
On a flat bed, Equation 4 reads |\nabla H| = \tau_b/(\rho_i g H). Multiplying by 2H and using \nabla(H^2) = 2H\,\nabla H,

\bigl|\nabla (H^2)\bigr| = \frac{2\tau_b}{\rho_i\, g}.
\tag{5}
The singularity is gone: the working variable w \equiv H^2 satisfies a plain eikonal |\nabla w| = G with a finite right-hand side G = 2\tau_b/(\rho_i g), and the boundary condition w = 0 at the margin is regular. Both solver modes work in w = H^2; the thickness is recovered as H = \sqrt{w} and the surface as z_s = H + z_b.

:flat mode
:flat solves Equation 5 exactly and drapes the result on the bed. It is the cheapest mode and is appropriate whenever bed relief is small compared with ice thickness (continental interiors). ICESHEET’s own sensitivity tests indicate the bed-gradient terms are second-order except in mountainous terrain.

title: Algorithms
text:
3. :full mode — the bed-gradient correction
Over significant relief the bed slope \nabla z_b enters the flux. Writing \nabla z_s = \nabla H + \nabla z_b in Equation 4 and carrying the algebra through in the w = H^2 variable gives, exactly in one dimension,

|\nabla w| = \sqrt{\,G^2 \;-\; 4\sqrt{w}\,(\nabla z_b \cdot \nabla w)\;-\;4\,w\,|\nabla z_b|^2\,},
\qquad G = \frac{2\tau_b}{\rho_i g}.
\tag{6}
Two properties make Equation 6 the right object to solve:

when \nabla z_b = 0 it reduces to the flat eikonal Equation 5, so :full and :flat agree exactly on a flat bed;
it is written in w = H^2, so it inherits the regular margin behaviour of Equation 5.

The effective right-hand side depends on \nabla w (through the \nabla z_b\cdot\nabla w term), so Equation 6 is not a pure eikonal. It is solved by an outer fixed-point iteration: freeze the right-hand side from the current w, solve the resulting constant-RHS eikonal for a candidate w_c, and update.

Damping is required
The naive update w \leftarrow \text{Eikonal}\!\left(\text{RHS}(w)\right) is not contractive — over real bed relief it oscillates indefinitely. The solver therefore uses an under-relaxed (damped) Picard iteration,

w^{(k+1)} = (1-\alpha)\, w^{(k)} + \alpha\, \text{Eikonal}\!\left(\text{RHS}(w^{(k)})\right),

with relaxation factor \alpha = \texttt{relax} (default 0.5). Damping converges the iteration to the same fixed point — i.e. to a vanishing residual (§6) — which is also what makes the implicit-differentiation gradient (§7) exact. Smaller \alpha is more robust on steep beds but needs more iterations; beds whose relief approaches the ice thickness over a few cells may not converge at all (lower relax, raise n_outer, refine the grid, or fall back to :flat).

title: Algorithms
text:
4. The Godunov update
The eikonal |\nabla u| = F is discretised cell-by-cell with the first-order Godunov upwind scheme. At a cell with grid spacings \Delta x, \Delta y, let a and b be the smaller of the two neighbour values along each axis:

a = \min(u_{i-1,j},\, u_{i+1,j}), \qquad b = \min(u_{i,j-1},\, u_{i,j+1}).

The updated value is the smallest u satisfying

\left(\frac{(u-a)_+}{\Delta x}\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{(u-b)_+}{\Delta y}\right)^{2} = F^2,
\qquad (\cdot)_+ = \max(\cdot, 0).

In practice: try the two-sided quadratic; accept it only if the result exceeds both neighbours (both upwind terms active); otherwise take the smaller one-sided update \min(a + F\,\Delta x,\; b + F\,\Delta y). This is godunov_eikonal, a pure function with no mutation or indexing — which is what lets the autodiff back ends differentiate it directly.

title: Algorithms
text:
5. Fast sweeping
The discrete eikonal is solved by the fast-sweeping method: Gauss–Seidel updates that keep the monotone minimum,

u_{i,j} \leftarrow \min\!\bigl(u_{i,j},\; \text{Godunov update}\bigr),

applied over the four diagonal traversal orderings (\nearrow, \nwarrow, \swarrow,
\searrow) in rotation. Because each ordering captures characteristics flowing in one quadrant of directions, a handful of sweeps converges the whole field in O(N) work, for any geometry. Interior cells start at +\infty; ice-free cells hold their boundary value (§6) so neighbour look-ups only ever read the working array.

title: Algorithms
text:
6. Boundary conditions
The margin is the interface between mask (grounded ice) and ice-free cells. Ice-free cells carry the Dirichlet value:

Land margin (z_b \ge z_{ss}): H = 0, i.e. w = 0.
Marine margin (z_b < z_{ss}, with z_{ss} the sea-surface elevation): the grounding line sits at the flotation thickness H_{flt} — the thickness whose weight balances the local water column, 
H_{flt} = \frac{\rho_w}{\rho_i}\,\bigl(z_{ss} - z_b\bigr), \qquad w = H_{flt}^2,
 reproducing ICESHEET’s marine grounding-line treatment. The eikonal then grows the ice inward from that thickness. No separate slope-limiting pre-pass is needed: the maximum surface slope is the plastic constraint the eikonal enforces.

title: Algorithms
text:
7. Differentiating with respect to \tau_b
Because the solver is a pure function of \tau_b, an objective on the output (a misfit to an observed surface, a target volume, …) is differentiable — enabling basal-shear-stress inversion.

Forward mode
For a scalar \tau_b or a low-dimensional parameterization, ForwardDiff differentiates straight through solve. Cost scales with the number of parameters.


Reverse mode (implicit differentiation)
For a full per-cell \tau_b field, differentiating through the sweeps would be wasteful. Instead, the converged field satisfies an optimality condition — the Godunov residual

r_{i,j}(w, \tau_b) = w_{i,j} - \bigl(\text{Godunov update at } (i,j)\bigr) = 0

on ice cells (and w - \text{bc} = 0 off-ice). By the implicit function theorem, for a loss L(w),

\frac{\partial L}{\partial \tau_b}
= -\,\frac{\partial L}{\partial w}\left(\frac{\partial r}{\partial w}\right)^{-1}\frac{\partial r}{\partial \tau_b},

so the gradient costs a single linear solve with the residual Jacobian, independent of the number of sweeps or outer iterations and scalable to a full field. The forward fast sweep is treated as a black box; only the residual is differentiated (with ForwardDiff internally). This is wired up with ImplicitDifferentiation.jl in a package extension, so any reverse-mode AD (e.g. Zygote) obtains the gradient.
The :flat gradient is exact to machine precision; the :full gradient is exact once the damped forward solve (§3) has driven the residual to \approx 0.






NoteNaN hygiene



\sqrt{w} has an infinite derivative at w=0. Wherever \sqrt{w} is differentiated it is floored at H_{\min}^2, because the unguarded \sqrt{0} derivative produces \infty\cdot 0
= \text{NaN} in the reverse pass (forward mode dodges this by branch selection).

title: Algorithms
text:
8. Velocity and surface mass balance
The plastic solve returns geometry and nothing else. By taking the perfectly-plastic (n\to\infty) limit it has discarded exactly the rate-dependent information that a rate factor, a velocity, and a surface mass balance (SMB) live in — the shape is rate-independent by construction. Those quantities are not hidden in the solution waiting to be read off; recovering them requires one extra closure layered on the fixed geometry.

The geometry already fixes the flow direction
The plastic balance Equation 3 is the membrane-free limit of the SSA/DIVA momentum balance: with \tau_d=\tau_b and the basal drag opposing flow, the longitudinal-stress-divergence term vanishes. Two consequences follow. First, the velocity is local and analytical — no elliptic solve. Second, ice flows down the surface gradient, along the characteristics of the eikonal, so the direction is known everywhere:

\hat{\mathbf u} = -\,\frac{\nabla z_s}{|\nabla z_s|}.
\tag{7}
What the geometry does not fix is the flux magnitude — a plastic bed sets the direction but not the speed. Closing that gap needs one assumption, and there are two dual ways to make it.


Closure B — parameters → velocity → SMB
Assume a rheology and a sliding law. The depth-averaged speed splits into internal deformation and basal sliding, both evaluated at the local driving stress \tau=\tau_b:

|\bar{\mathbf u}| = \underbrace{u_b(\tau_b)}_{\text{sliding law}}
\;+\; \underbrace{\frac{2A}{n+2}\,\tau_b^{\,n}\,H}_{\text{SIA deformation}} .
\tag{8}
The deformational term is the SIA closed form with the plastic constraint \tau_d=\tau_b substituted; the sliding term inverts the chosen law for the speed that produces drag \tau_b (basal_velocity). For a regularized-Coulomb bed at its Coulomb cap the sliding speed is genuinely undetermined and is returned as NaN rather than fabricated. With \bar{\mathbf u}=|\bar{\mathbf u}|\,\hat{\mathbf u} in hand, steady-state mass continuity gives the implied SMB as a flux divergence:

\dot b = \nabla\!\cdot\mathbf q = \nabla\!\cdot(\bar{\mathbf u}\,H).
\tag{9}
This is diva_velocity followed by smb_from_velocity. Because the velocity is a smooth function of \tau_b, so is the SMB — the whole closure doubles as an observation operator for \tau_b inversion (§7).


Closure A — SMB → velocity
The dual. Prescribe \dot b and route it down the fixed surface to recover the flux the ice must carry to be in steady state — the balance velocity (Budd & Warner 1996):

\nabla\!\cdot\mathbf q = \dot b, \qquad \mathbf q \parallel \hat{\mathbf u}, \qquad
|\bar{\mathbf u}| = \frac{|\mathbf q|}{H}.
\tag{10}
This is the rigorous reading when \tau_b is a basal sliding resistance, where the speed is non-local — set by upstream accumulation, not local rheology. It is balance_flux / balance_velocity.


One discrete flux operator
Closures A and B are exact inverses in the continuum, and the implementation preserves that discretely. Both are built on a single finite-volume multiple-flow-direction (MFD) operator: a cell’s throughput is partitioned to its lower grounded neighbours in proportion to their surface drop. The routing (balance_flux, source → throughput) and the implied-SMB divergence (smb_from_velocity, throughput → source) use the same weights, so they are adjoint and

\texttt{balance\_flux}\bigl(\texttt{smb\_from\_velocity}(\bar{\mathbf u})\bigr) = \bar{\mathbf u}\,H

holds to machine precision. Routing processes cells from highest surface to lowest, so every upstream contribution has arrived before a cell distributes its throughput; flux delivered to the margin terminates there (the catchment discharge), and mass is conserved exactly. This consistency is not cosmetic — it is what makes the SMB-driven reconstruction (§9) have a clean fixed point.


Consistency: where A and B meet
Subtracting the analytical deformation Equation 8 from the balance speed Equation 10 leaves the basal velocity the geometry and SMB jointly demand,

u_{\text{basal}} = u_{\text{balance}} - u_d,
\tag{11}
(implied_basal_velocity). Inverting a sliding law at this u_{\text{basal}} and the local \tau_b gives the bed friction implied by the reconstruction — a check on whether the plastic geometry plus an assumed climate is consistent with a plausible bed. A negative value flags a cell where the balance flux is smaller than internal deformation alone would carry.

title: Algorithms
Algorithms
text:
10. What was deliberately dropped
Relative to ICESHEET 2.0, the grid Hamilton–Jacobi solver makes the following machinery unnecessary: method-of-characteristics flowline tracing, crossover detection (the motorcycle-graph algorithm), saddle/dome detection and polygon splitting, the GIA / sea-level iteration loop, and the GMT projection/preprocessing pipeline. See the Design notes for the full accounting.

title: Inverting basal shear stress
text:
The plastic solve turns a margin, a bed, and a basal-shear-stress field \tau_b into an ice sheet. \tau_b carries essentially all of the reconstruction’s freedom, so the practical question is the inverse one: what \tau_b makes the reconstruction match data? This page covers how that inverse problem is posed, the two parameterizations of \tau_b the examples provide (and how to switch between them), and how this relates to — and automates — the hand-tuned shear-stress domains of ICESHEET (Gowan et al. 2016, 2021).
The gradient machinery itself (implicit differentiation of the converged eikonal) lives in Algorithms §7; here we use it.

title: Inverting basal shear stress
text:
The inverse problem
Pick an objective on the reconstruction and minimise it over \tau_b. For present-day ice the natural target is the observed surface, through surface_misfit:

\min_{\tau_b}\; \big\langle\, (z_s(\tau_b) - z_s^{\text{obs}})^2 \,\big\rangle_{\text{mask}},
\qquad z_s(\tau_b) = z_b + H(\tau_b).

differentiable_thickness gives \partial H/\partial\tau_b by reverse-mode AD, so any gradient optimiser (the examples use Adam) drives the misfit down. The grounded mask is the prescribed margin — an ICESHEET input, not something inverted — so the recovered ice extent always equals the mask; only the geometry inside it is fit.
The number of free parameters in \tau_b should match what the target data can resolve. A dense present-day surface constrains a dense field; a sparse paleo target (relative sea level) constrains only a handful of regional values. The two parameterizations below sit at those two ends.

title: Inverting basal shear stress
text:
Two parameterizations
The example examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl implements both and switches between them with a single environment variable, METHOD:
julia --project=examples examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl                  # METHOD=tiled (default)
METHOD=percell julia --project=examples examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl   # point-by-point
Everything downstream — the objective, the Adam loop, the outputs — is shared; only the map from optimisation parameters to the \tau_b field differs.

Point-by-point (METHOD=percell)
A full per-cell \tau_b field, regularized to a length scale REG_KM by a Gaussian-blur reparameterization: optimise a raw field \varphi and set

\tau_b = \operatorname{clamp}\big(K_x\,\varphi\,K_y\big),

with K_x, K_y separable 1-D Gaussian smoothers (\sigma = \texttt{REG\_KM}/\Delta x cells). Being linear, the blur differentiates cleanly under Zygote, and it guarantees \tau_b varies only on scales \gtrsim REG_KM regardless of the data fit. Many effective degrees of freedom; fits a dense observed surface closely (on Greenland GRL-32KM, ~85 m rmse).


Tiled (METHOD=tiled, default)
A low-dimensional, paleo-transferable parameterization — the automatic version of ICESHEET’s hand-tuned shear-stress domains. Cells are grouped into tiles defined only from data available for an unknown ice sheet: bedrock roughness (windowed standard deviation of z_b, binned into N_ROUGH quantile classes) crossed with a sediment mask (z_sed, split soft/hard at SED_THRESH). The ice thickness and ice-derived drainage basins are never used to draw the tiles. Each tile d carries one scalar \theta_d:

\tau_b(\mathbf x) = \operatorname{clamp}\Big(\textstyle\sum_d \theta_d\, \mathbb 1[\,\mathbf x \in d\,]\Big),

embedded via a one-hot selector matrix (linear, hence Zygote-friendly). The default is N_ROUGH = 4 × 2 sediment classes = 8 tiles. The observed surface enters only as the calibration target — exactly the modern-surface calibration Gowan used for Greenland and Antarctica, and the slot a relative-sea-level objective would occupy for a paleo reconstruction.

title: Inverting basal shear stress
section: Relation to ICESHEET / Gowan et al.
text:
Relation to ICESHEET / Gowan et al.
ICESHEET (Gowan et al. 2016) is the same perfectly-plastic, equilibrium forward model. In PaleoMIST (Gowan et al. 2021) \tau_b was chosen by:

Target. Relative sea level through a GIA model for the paleo ice sheets (adjust \tau_b to maximise LGM volume, reduce it through deglaciation to fit postglacial sea-level indicators), plus geological margins; and the modern surface for Greenland and Antarctica.
Domains. Defined by topographic variability (mountainous vs flat) refined by surficial geology (continuous sediment → low \tau_b; hard/discontinuous → high), with boundaries on fjords, mountain ranges, and the flat interior.
Tuning. Manual and iterative — repeated by hand until the fit was acceptable.

The tiled method reproduces this logic but automates both halves: the domains come from bed roughness + sediment programmatically, and the per-domain values come from gradient descent rather than hand-tuning. On Greenland it recovers exactly Gowan’s premise — soft- sediment tiles collapse to 5–32 kPa, hard-bed tiles sit at 60–130 kPa.

title: Inverting basal shear stress
section: How many tiles? A resolution test
text:
How many tiles? A resolution test
The one judgement ICESHEET left to intuition — how many domains — can be made quantitative. examples/invert_taub_sweep_greenland.jl sweeps the tile count and plots surface-misfit rmse against the number of free parameters; the knee of that curve is the data-supported resolution. On Greenland GRL-32KM:

Sediment geology dominates, not roughness count. A single hard/soft split (2 tiles) drops rmse from 514 → 356 m — more than eight roughness-only tiles achieve (446 m).
The knee is early — ~6–8 tiles. Beyond it the curve flattens (6 tiles → 333 m, 15 tiles → 327 m): extra parameters do not earn their keep.
An irreducible floor. Even the best tiling (~327 m) sits well above the per-cell fit (~85 m), so a substantial part of the surface signal reflects \tau_b variability that static bed roughness and sediment cannot explain (basal thermal/hydrological state).

title: Inverting basal shear stress
text:
Conditioning note: :flat vs :full
The inversions use mode = :flat. The :full adjoint forms a dense Jacobian over the whole grid, whose direct linear solve becomes singular/ill-posed on large grids; :flat gives a well-conditioned eikonal adjoint and ties \tau_b directly to surface slope, which is the more natural object when fitting a surface. For finer grids the per-cell gradient cost is dominated by that dense solve — see the differentiable_thickness docstring for the iterative-solver option.

title: Inverting basal shear stress
text:
Toward a paleo (RSL) reconstruction
The tiled parameterization is the bridge to a genuine paleo reconstruction: keep the bed-derived tiles, and replace the observed-surface objective with relative sea level routed through a GIA model. Because the tiles need no ice-sheet knowledge to define, the same setup transfers to an ice sheet whose geometry is unknown — which is exactly the regime ICESHEET was built for, and where reducing the parameter count is not optional but required.

title: Usage
text:
using Pkg
Pkg.add(url = "https://github.com/fesmc/PlasticIceSheet.jl")
The core package depends only on LinearAlgebra. Reverse-mode differentiation and NetCDF I/O are loaded on demand through package extensions (below).

title: Usage
text:
using Pkg
Pkg.add(url = "https://github.com/fesmc/PlasticIceSheet.jl")
The core package depends only on LinearAlgebra. Reverse-mode differentiation and NetCDF I/O are loaded on demand through package extensions (below).

title: Usage
text:
Solving
using PlasticIceSheet

z_s, H = solve(z_b, τ_b, mask; dx, dy, mode = :full)







argument
meaning




z_b
bed elevation z_b (m), nx × ny matrix


τ_b
basal shear stress (Pa); a scalar is broadcast to a uniform field


mask
true where grounded ice exists; the margin is the mask boundary


dx, dy
grid spacings (m)



Key options (see the docstring for the rest):

mode — :full (default, full Hamilton–Jacobi over arbitrary relief) or :flat (flat-bed eikonal, cheapest; exact when relief ≪ thickness).
params — a PlasticParams (densities ρ_i/ρ_w, gravity g, sea level z_ss, H_min).
n_outer, outer_tol, relax — :full damped fixed-point controls. relax ∈ (0,1] is the under-relaxation factor (default 0.5); lower is more robust on steep beds but slower.

Returns (z_s, H) — surface elevation z_s and thickness H; ice-free cells have H = 0, z_s = z_b.

Diagnostics
ice_volume(H, dx, dy)                # m³
ice_area(mask, dx, dy)               # m²
surface_misfit(z_s, z_s_obs, mask)   # mean-squared surface misfit over ice

title: Usage
text:
Velocity and surface mass balance
The plastic solve returns only geometry. A DIVA closure layered on the fixed geometry recovers a depth-averaged velocity and a first-order SMB — see Algorithms §8 for the theory. Pick a rheology and a sliding law:
rheology = GlenRheology(A = 1.0e-16, n = 3.0)           # isothermal Glen
sliding  = WeertmanSliding(β = 1.0e4, m = 3.0)          # also LinearSliding(β),
                                                        # RegularizedCoulomb(C, u_0, m)

Closure B — velocity, then implied SMB
vel = diva_velocity(z_s, H, τ_b, mask, dx, dy; rheology, sliding)
# named tuple: ux, uy, speed, u_b (sliding), u_def (deformation), ex, ey (flow direction)

smb = smb_from_velocity(vel, H, z_s, mask, dx, dy)      # ḃ = ∇·(ūH)
RegularizedCoulomb returns a NaN basal speed wherever τ_b ≥ C (bed at the Coulomb cap, sliding speed undetermined) — those cells should take their speed from closure A.


Closure A — balance velocity from a prescribed SMB
bal = balance_velocity(z_s, H, mask, smb, dx, dy)       # named tuple: speed, ux, uy, Q, ...
balance_flux (routing) is the exact inverse of smb_from_velocity, so feeding closure B’s SMB back through closure A recovers closure B’s velocity to machine precision.


Consistency — the implied bed
ib = implied_basal_velocity(z_s, H, τ_b, mask, smb, dx, dy; rheology)
# u_basal = u_balance − u_def: the basal velocity the geometry + SMB demand
Inverting a sliding law at ib.u_basal and the local τ_b gives the friction implied by the reconstruction — a check on whether the geometry and assumed climate are consistent with a plausible bed.


SMB-driven reconstruction
The dual of solve: drive the geometry with an SMB instead of a yield stress, on the same fixed margin (see Algorithms §9).
z_s, H = diva_reconstruct(z_b, mask, smb, dx, dy; rheology, sliding)






Warning



The outer iteration is non-contractive (like :full) and is under-relaxed; the default relax = 0.05 converges the test configurations, but stiffer or finer grids need a smaller relax and more n_outer. A complete free-margin steady state is out of scope — use a dynamic model (e.g. Yelmo) for that.

title: Usage
text:
Inverting for \tau_b
The solver is differentiable with respect to \tau_b. See Algorithms §7 for the theory.

Forward mode — scalar / low-dimensional \tau_b
using ForwardDiff
loss(τ_b) = surface_misfit(first(solve(z_b, τ_b, mask; dx, dy)), z_s_obs, mask)
g = ForwardDiff.derivative(loss, 1.0e5)


Reverse mode — full per-cell \tau_b field
Load the extension triggers, then differentiate differentiable_thickness with any reverse-mode AD:
using ImplicitDifferentiation, ADTypes, ForwardDiff, Zygote

loss(τ_b) = surface_misfit(differentiable_thickness(τ_b, z_b, mask; dx, dy) .+ z_b, z_s_obs, mask)
g = Zygote.gradient(loss, τ_b)[1]      # gradient w.r.t. the whole τ_b field
A complete, runnable Adam-based recovery of a spatially-varying \tau_b from an observed surface is in examples/invert_tau.jl. It drives the misfit down by four orders of magnitude and recovers the two-region truth to within a fraction of a kPa.


Choosing a parameterization
How you parameterize \tau_b — a regularized per-cell field, or a few bed-derived tiles — matters as much as the gradient. The real-data Greenland example examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl implements both and switches with one env var:
julia --project=examples examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl                  # tiled (default)
METHOD=percell julia --project=examples examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl   # point-by-point
See Inversion for the parameterizations, the link to ICESHEET’s hand-tuned shear-stress domains, and a resolution test for the number of tiles.






Tip



The default reverse-mode linear solver is direct (robust for modest grids). For very large fields, pass an iterative solver — see the differentiable_thickness docstring.

title: Usage
text:
NetCDF I/O
using NCDatasets

inp = load_plastic_inputs("inputs.nc")             # (; z_b, τ_b, mask, dx, dy, x, y)
z_s, H = solve(inp.z_b, inp.τ_b, inp.mask; inp.dx, inp.dy)
save_reconstruction("out.nc", z_s, H; x = inp.x, y = inp.y)

title: Usage
text:
Caveats

Steady-state geometry only — biased thick/peaked where the real ice was streaming or dynamic (low effective driving stress the plastic assumption cannot see).
\tau_b carries essentially all the uncertainty. For a first guess a constant (~50–100 kPa) or a bed-keyed value is fine; do not over-interpret the result as a constrained reconstruction.
Very steep beds (relief approaching the ice thickness over a few cells) may not converge in :full — see Algorithms §3.

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl
text:
PlasticIceSheet.jl reconstructs a perfectly-plastic, steady-state ice sheet from three inputs — a grounded-ice margin/mask, a bed elevation z_b, and a basal shear stress field \tau_b — and returns a glaciologically plausible ice surface z_s and thickness H. There is no time-stepping, no climate, and no ice dynamics.
It is a clean-room simplification and modernization of ICESHEET 2.0 by Evan Gowan and colleagues — source code, the method’s description paper (Gowan et al., 2016, Geosci. Model Dev.), and its global application to the past 80,000 years (Gowan et al., 2021, Nat. Commun.). The core physics — a single published equation — is retained, while ICESHEET’s recursive flowline / crossover / saddle machinery is replaced by a grid eikonal solver (see Origin).
It is meant as a complementary first-guess tool next to a full dynamic model (e.g. Yelmo) and an independent GIA / sea-level solver: to seed a spin-up, force a surface-mass-balance model, or serve as a relaxation target.

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl
text:
The idea
Under a perfectly-plastic, steady-state assumption, the basal shear stress \tau_b balances the gravitational driving stress \tau_d everywhere:

\tau_b = \tau_d .

The driving stress is fixed by the ice geometry — for surface elevation z_s, bed elevation z_b, and thickness H = z_s - z_b, \tau_d = \rho_i\, g\, H\, |\nabla z_s| — so the prescribed \tau_b relates to the surface as \tau_b = \rho_i\, g\, H\, |\nabla z_s|. Rearranging for the surface slope gives the equation we solve:

|\nabla z_s| = \frac{\tau_b}{\rho_i\, g\, H} = \frac{\tau_b}{\rho_i\, g\, (z_s - z_b)} .

This is a static Hamilton–Jacobi (eikonal) equation. Solving it with an upwind grid scheme means saddles, domes, and coalescing ice caps emerge automatically from the viscosity solution — there is no flowline tracing, crossover detection, or saddle bookkeeping. See Algorithms for the full derivation and numerics.
An optional DIVA closure layered on the fixed geometry recovers what perfect plasticity discards — a depth-averaged velocity and a first-order surface mass balance — and its dual can drive the geometry from an SMB instead of a yield stress (Algorithms §8–9).

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl
text:
Quick start
using PlasticIceSheet

nx, ny = 100, 100
dx = dy = 2000.0                                   # grid spacing (m)
bed  = zeros(nx, ny)                               # bed elevation B (m)
mask = [hypot(i-50.5, j-50.5) <= 40 for i in 1:nx, j in 1:ny]
τ_b    = 1.0e5                                       # basal shear stress (Pa)

E, H = solve(bed, τ_b, mask; dx, dy, mode = :full)     # :full (default) or :flat
A full Hamilton–Jacobi solve on a 300×300 grid takes ~0.1 s. The basal shear stress \tau_b is differentiable, enabling gradient-based inversion.

title: PlasticIceSheet.jl
text:
Origin
This is a clean-room simplification and modernization of ICESHEET 2.0 (Gowan et al. 2016, GMD; Gowan et al. 2021, Nat. Commun.). The core physics — one published equation — is retained; the ~2,600-line recursive flowline / crossover / saddle machinery is replaced by a grid eikonal solver on the order of a couple hundred lines. The Design notes record what was kept, simplified, and dropped, and why.
PlasticIceSheet.jl is MIT-licensed.
